title: Trait Ecology Initiative Malaysia
text:
Note



This page is still under construction. Please feel free to email me or fill in this interest form to discuss further or know more.


The conservation of our dwindling biodiversity demands rapid information gathering, yet we inefficiently assess the risks of our 170,000 species one-by-one in Malaysia. The solution is trait ecology that synthesises discrete species into continuous life-history spectra, but we are also slow to leverage transferable knowledge from this growing and exciting field. There is an urgent need to modernise our mode of operation.
We need to grow trait ecology in Malaysia as a holistic programme that integrates generalisable findings into conservation, mobilises knowledge with a living database, and establishes a data-equity system for long-term capacity building.
I am drafting a working or interest group to achieve these goals, tentatively named the Trait Ecology Initiative Malaysia (TEIM). I really want to say “we” but currently it’s just myself. Please feel free to fill in this form to indicate your interest. Let’s organise a meeting once we have reached a critical mass!

title: Publications
text:
Please see Google Scholar or ResearchGate, who are better than me at upkeeping the publication list.
(I might curate this publication list one day, if I can find time…)

title: LAI Hao Ran
text:
Apa khabar! My name is Lai Hao Ran (賴浩然) and a plant community ecologist. My research aims to understand and generalise the dynamics of forest biodiversity through functional traits, statistical rigour, and long-term monitoring.  My career goal is to build capacity and foster a more innovative research community in Malaysia and tropical Asia.
Please feel free to reach out for research collaboration, forest fieldwork, guest teaching, and project (co)supervision.


Ecologist / Data scientist, South East Asia Rainforest Research Partnership (SEARRP), Malaysia 



2019–2025 | Post-doctoral fellow, School of Biological Sciences, University of Canterbury, New Zealand 
2019 | Visiting Research Fellow, Centre for Urban Greenery and Ecology, National Parks Board, Singapore 
2015–2018 | Teaching assistant, National University of Singapore, Singapore  



2015–2018 | PhD, Department of Biological Sciences, National University of Singapore, Singapore 
2009–2012 | BSc (Hons), School of Biological Sciences, The University of Queensland, Australia

title: MYtraits: a living trait database for the flora of Malaysia
text:
Note



The MYtraits database and R package are coming soon. Thank you for your patience as it is currently a personal endeavor without much funding. Please feel free to email me to discuss further or know more.





An accessible database is crucial to coordinate fundamental research, species management, ecosystem restoration and public mainstreaming. I am building the MYtraits database that is integrated with the statistical programming software R .

title: Reminder
text:
Ursula K. Le Guin (1929–2018) writes science fictions that are strangely beautiful. Maybe it isn’t too strange that they contain memorable lines that are strangely applicable to science, and life in general. Here are two from The Left Hand of Darkness (1969):

To learn which questions are unanswerable, and not to answer them: this skill is most needful in times of stress and darkness.


When action grows unprofitable, gather information; when information grows unprofitable, sleep.

And to add another from Robert A. Heinlein’s (1907–1988) Starship Troopers (1959), which strangely coincided with the birth of our child at the time of reading:

Each year we gain a little. You have to keep a sense of proportion.

Echoing Mara van der Lugt’s Hopeful Pessimism (2025), these reminds one to stay strong while building capacity in a messy and crowded world.
